---
title: "The differentiable-programming paradigm"
---

The last two chapters were about mechanism: a derivative is a linear map
([differentiation](differentiation.qmd)), and you apply it matrix-free, exploiting
its structure, on real kernels ([three kernels](three-kernels.qmd)). This chapter
steps back to ask what the mechanism is *for*. The answer is a way of writing
programs — **differentiable programming** — in which you do not spell out the steps
that solve a problem; you specify a *family* of candidate solutions and an
*objective*, and let automatic differentiation and an optimiser *find* the solution.
Deep learning is the famous instance, but the idea is more general, and it is the
reason `nitrix` exists: a library of differentiable primitives turns a scientific
computation into a program you can optimise.

This chapter is deliberately balanced. Differentiable programming is powerful and it
is constrained, and an honest account states both. We cover the paradigm and its
three-step loop; the program-as-graph that underlies it; why *automatic*
differentiation beats the numerical and symbolic alternatives; the differentiability
constraint and the standard ways to bend it; how real frameworks implement all this;
and where the approach runs out of road — which is exactly what the final two
chapters take up.

## From writing instructions to specifying objectives {#sec-paradigm}

In ordinary programming you write explicit instructions: a sequence of steps that, if
correct, computes the answer. Differentiable programming — sometimes called "software
2.0" — replaces the *hardest-to-specify* steps with a different contract, in three
parts:

1. **A hypothesis class.** Choose a *family* of differentiable functions
   $f(\,\cdot\,;\theta)$ parameterised by $\theta$, rich enough to contain a good
   solution to the problem. (In deep learning the family is a neural-network
   architecture; in `nitrix` it might be a parameterised registration warp, a
   smoothing operator with a learnable scale, or a whole processing workflow with
   tunable knobs.)
2. **An objective.** Write a differentiable scalar $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ that scores
   how well a given $\theta$ meets the goal — small when the program does what you
   want.
3. **Optimise by differentiation.** Use automatic differentiation to get
   $\nabla_\theta\mathcal{L}$ and hand it to an optimiser, which adjusts $\theta$ to
   reduce the objective. Repeat until satisfied.

The work shifts from *how* to *what*: instead of programming the solution, you
program a measure of success and a space to search, and gradients do the searching.
@fig-dp-loop is the loop this produces — the training loop of deep learning, but
drawn generically.

::: {#fig-dp-loop}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 250" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="The differentiable-programming loop: parameters feed a model that makes a prediction, an objective scores it, reverse-mode AD computes the gradient of the objective with respect to the parameters, and an optimiser updates them; repeat."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="dpf" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#2c6a45"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="dpb" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#8a3ffc"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="40"  y="40" width="150" height="56" rx="6" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="115" y="64" text-anchor="middle">parameters θ</text>
  <text x="115" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">the hypothesis class</text>
  <rect x="300" y="40" width="160" height="56" rx="6" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="380" y="64" text-anchor="middle">model f(x; θ)</text>
  <text x="380" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">prediction</text>
  <rect x="570" y="40" width="150" height="56" rx="6" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="645" y="64" text-anchor="middle">objective ℒ(θ)</text>
  <text x="645" y="82" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">a scalar score</text>
  <line x1="190" y1="68" x2="298" y2="68" stroke="#2c6a45" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#dpf)"/>
  <line x1="460" y1="68" x2="568" y2="68" stroke="#2c6a45" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#dpf)"/>
  <text x="380" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#2c6a45">forward pass</text>
  <!-- backward -->
  <rect x="300" y="160" width="160" height="56" rx="6" fill="#f3eefb" stroke="#8a3ffc"/>
  <text x="380" y="184" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">∇θ ℒ  (reverse-mode AD)</text>
  <text x="380" y="202" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#8a3ffc">the gradient</text>
  <line x1="600" y1="96" x2="445" y2="158" stroke="#8a3ffc" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#dpb)"/>
  <text x="540" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#8a3ffc">backward pass</text>
  <line x1="300" y1="188" x2="150" y2="98" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#dpf)"/>
  <text x="190" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">optimiser updates θ</text>
</svg>

The differentiable-programming loop. Parameters drive a model to a prediction; an
objective scores it (forward, green); reverse-mode AD computes the gradient of the
objective with respect to the parameters (backward, purple); an optimiser nudges
$\theta$ downhill; repeat. It is the deep-learning training loop, but nothing in it
is special to neural networks — any differentiable family and objective fit.
:::

## The program is a graph {#sec-graph}

What makes step 3 possible is the object from the [JAX/XLA chapter](xla-jax.qmd#sec-trace):
the traced program, viewed as a **computational graph**. Its nodes are primitive
operations and its edges are the tensors that flow between them; the *sources* are
the parameters and inputs, and the *sink* is the objective (@fig-dp-graph). The same
trace JAX compiles for speed is the one it differentiates — recorded once, as a
sequence of primitive assignments (a *Wengert list*), each with a known local
derivative.

This view makes the one hard rule precise: the **differentiability constraint**.
For a gradient to flow from the objective back to a parameter, *every node on a path
between them must be differentiable*. A single non-differentiable operation on that
path — a hard threshold, an `argmax`, a draw from a random distribution — severs the
gradient, and the optimiser goes blind to whatever lies upstream. Much of the craft
of differentiable programming is keeping those paths intact, the subject of
[the relaxation section](#sec-relaxation).

::: {#fig-dp-graph}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 250" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="A computational graph: parameter and input tensors at the sources flow rightward through primitive operations to a scalar loss at the sink; the gradient flows leftward back along the same graph."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="12">
  <defs>
    <marker id="gf" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#2c6a45"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="gb" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#8a3ffc"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- sources (tensors = diamonds) -->
  <g font-size="11">
    <polygon points="40,70 70,55 100,70 70,85" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="70" y="74" text-anchor="middle">x</text>
    <polygon points="40,130 70,115 100,130 70,145" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="70" y="134" text-anchor="middle">W</text>
    <polygon points="40,185 70,170 100,185 70,200" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="70" y="189" text-anchor="middle">b</text>
  </g>
  <!-- primitives (circles) -->
  <circle cx="210" cy="105" r="28" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="210" y="109" text-anchor="middle">matmul</text>
  <circle cx="350" cy="120" r="26" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="350" y="124" text-anchor="middle">+ b</text>
  <circle cx="480" cy="120" r="26" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="480" y="124" text-anchor="middle">relu</text>
  <circle cx="610" cy="120" r="30" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="610" y="124" text-anchor="middle">loss ℒ</text>
  <!-- forward edges -->
  <g stroke="#2c6a45" stroke-width="1.4">
    <line x1="100" y1="70"  x2="184" y2="98"  marker-end="url(#gf)"/>
    <line x1="100" y1="130" x2="184" y2="112" marker-end="url(#gf)"/>
    <line x1="238" y1="108" x2="326" y2="118" marker-end="url(#gf)"/>
    <line x1="100" y1="185" x2="326" y2="130" marker-end="url(#gf)"/>
    <line x1="376" y1="120" x2="452" y2="120" marker-end="url(#gf)"/>
    <line x1="506" y1="120" x2="580" y2="120" marker-end="url(#gf)"/>
  </g>
  <!-- backward sweep -->
  <path d="M 610,150 C 400,230 250,230 70,205" fill="none" stroke="#8a3ffc" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="5,4" marker-end="url(#gb)"/>
  <text x="380" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#8a3ffc">gradient flows back along the same graph</text>
  <text x="70" y="40" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sources: params + input</text>
  <text x="610" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sink: scalar objective</text>
</svg>

The computational graph. Diamonds are tensors, circles are primitive operations;
the forward pass (green) flows from parameter and input sources to the scalar loss,
and the gradient (purple, dashed) flows back along the very same graph. Every node
on a source-to-sink path must be differentiable for the gradient to reach the
parameter.
:::

## Why automatic — not numerical or symbolic {#sec-vs}

There are three ways to get a derivative from a program, and it is worth seeing why
the paradigm settled on the third.

**Numerical differentiation** perturbs an input and measures the response:
$\partial f/\partial x_j \approx [f(x+he_j)-f(x)]/h$. It is trivial to implement and
useful for *spot-checking* a hand-derived gradient (we used it on the
[gemm formulas](three-kernels.qmd#sec-gemm)). But it is unfit for training, for two
reasons. It costs one full re-evaluation of the program *per parameter* — hopeless
when $\theta$ has millions of entries. And it is inherently **inexact**: too large a
step $h$ incurs truncation error, too small a step incurs floating-point rounding
error, and the two cannot be minimised at once. @fig-dp-finitediff is the classic
picture of that bind.

```{python}
#| label: fig-dp-finitediff
#| fig-cap: "Why numerical differentiation is fragile. The error of a forward finite-difference estimate of d/dx sin(x), as a function of step size h (log–log). Large h is dominated by truncation error (∝ h, the right slope); small h by floating-point rounding error (∝ ε/h, the left slope). The best achievable error sits at the valley near h ≈ √ε ≈ 10⁻⁸ and is far from machine precision — whereas automatic differentiation is exact to rounding at any scale."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x0 = 1.0
exact = np.cos(x0)                      # d/dx sin(x) = cos(x)
h = np.logspace(-16, 0, 300)
fd = (np.sin(x0 + h) - np.sin(x0)) / h  # forward finite difference
err = np.abs(fd - exact)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.0))
ax.loglog(h, err, color="#b03b4b", lw=2)
ax.axvline(np.sqrt(np.finfo(float).eps), color="0.6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
ax.text(2e-8, 1e-3, r"valley at $h\approx\sqrt{\varepsilon}$", fontsize=9, color="0.3")
ax.text(1e-5, 3e-6, "truncation\n∝ h", fontsize=8, color="#7a2b36", ha="left")
ax.text(1e-13, 3e-4, "rounding\n∝ ε/h", fontsize=8, color="#7a2b36", ha="left")
ax.set_xlabel("finite-difference step  h")
ax.set_ylabel("error  |estimate − cos(1)|")
ax.set_title("Numerical differentiation: truncation vs rounding")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Symbolic differentiation** manipulates the *expression* to produce a closed-form
derivative — exact, but prone to **expression swell**: rules like the product rule
duplicate subexpressions, so the derivative formula can grow far faster than the
original program, and it returns a formula rather than efficiently-evaluated numbers.

**Automatic differentiation** is the synthesis. It works on the *trace* of the
program, applying each primitive's known derivative and reusing the intermediates
already computed (the graph shares subexpressions, so there is no swell). It is exact
to floating point, like symbolic differentiation, and — for a scalar objective — it
delivers the entire gradient in a single reverse pass, regardless of the number of
parameters, as the [differentiation chapter](differentiation.qmd#sec-cost)
established. The table summarises the trade.

| | numerical | symbolic | **automatic** |
|---|---|---|---|
| exactness | approximate (step-dependent) | exact | exact (to rounding) |
| cost for a scalar loss of $n$ params | $O(n)$ re-evaluations | — (a formula) | **one reverse pass** |
| failure mode | truncation vs rounding | expression swell | (memory of the tape) |
| returns | numbers | a formula | numbers |

That AD is the same *trace reinterpretation* as compilation — [Thread A](index.qmd#sec-thread-trace)
— is why a single framework can both `jit` and `grad` a program.

## The differentiability constraint, and how to bend it {#sec-relaxation}

Many useful operations are not differentiable: choosing the largest element
(`argmax`), sampling from a distribution, sorting, rounding, a hard `if`. Each
severs the gradient. The paradigm has three standard ways to keep a path open, and
two of them you have already met.

- **Smooth relaxation.** Replace the hard operation with a smooth surrogate that
  approaches it in a limit. The `softmax` is the smooth `argmax`; the
  [LOG semiring](three-kernels.qmd#sec-semiring) is the smooth `min`/`max`; there are
  soft sorts and soft top-$k$. The relaxation has a temperature that trades
  faithfulness against gradient quality — sharper is closer to the hard op but has
  flatter, less useful gradients.
- **Reparameterisation.** When the non-differentiable step is *sampling*, move the
  randomness into a fixed, parameter-free noise input, so the parameters enter only
  through differentiable arithmetic. To sample $z\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$,
  write $z=\mu+\sigma\,\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ drawn
  *outside* the gradient path; now $\partial z/\partial\mu$ and
  $\partial z/\partial\sigma$ exist (@fig-dp-reparam). This is the trick that makes
  variational autoencoders trainable, and it sits naturally in `nitrix`, where
  randomness is already an explicit `jax.random` key passed in, not hidden state.
- **Surrogate gradients.** Use the hard operation on the forward pass but substitute
  a chosen smooth gradient on the backward pass (the "straight-through" estimator).
  Pragmatic, and deliberately inconsistent — the backward pass differentiates a
  *different* function than the forward computes — so it is a tool of last resort.

::: {#fig-dp-reparam}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="Reparameterisation: sampling z from N(mu, sigma squared) blocks the gradient, but writing z as mu plus sigma times a fixed standard-normal noise lets the gradient flow to mu and sigma."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="12">
  <defs>
    <marker id="rpf" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="190" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">blocked</text>
  <rect x="60" y="50" width="120" height="44" rx="6" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="120" y="76" text-anchor="middle">μ, σ</text>
  <rect x="240" y="50" width="130" height="44" rx="6" fill="#f3dede" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="305" y="70" text-anchor="middle">z ~ N(μ, σ²)</text>
  <text x="305" y="86" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#b03b4b">sampling</text>
  <line x1="180" y1="72" x2="238" y2="72" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#rpf)"/>
  <text x="305" y="120" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#b03b4b">✗ gradient cannot flow through the draw</text>
  <text x="600" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">reparameterised</text>
  <rect x="470" y="50" width="90" height="44" rx="6" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="515" y="76" text-anchor="middle">μ, σ</text>
  <rect x="470" y="120" width="90" height="40" rx="6" fill="#eef0f2" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="515" y="145" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11">ε ~ N(0,1)</text>
  <rect x="620" y="74" width="120" height="44" rx="6" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="680" y="100" text-anchor="middle">z = μ + σ·ε</text>
  <line x1="560" y1="72" x2="618" y2="88" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#rpf)"/>
  <line x1="560" y1="140" x2="618" y2="108" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#rpf)"/>
  <text x="600" y="185" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#2c6a45">✓ gradient flows to μ, σ; randomness isolated in ε</text>
</svg>

Reparameterisation. A draw $z\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ (left) blocks the
gradient. Rewriting it as $z=\mu+\sigma\,\varepsilon$ with a fixed standard-normal
$\varepsilon$ (right) isolates the randomness in a parameter-free input, so the
parameters $\mu,\sigma$ enter only through differentiable arithmetic and the
gradient flows.
:::

## How frameworks implement it {#sec-implementations}

Two implementation strategies dominate, and the difference is exactly the
[trace question](xla-jax.qmd#sec-trace) from earlier.

**Operator overloading** (PyTorch). Tensors carry a flag; when it is set, every
operation on them is overloaded to *also* record itself, its inputs, and the material
needed for its backward rule onto a tape, building the computational graph
*dynamically* along the exact path the Python code takes. The backward pass then
walks that tape. This **eager** style is flexible — ordinary Python control flow just
works, because the graph is whatever ran — but the dynamism limits whole-program
optimisation, since there is no static graph to analyse ahead of time.

**Trace / source transformation** (JAX, TensorFlow 1). Trace the pure function once
to a static graph (`jaxpr`), then *transform* it — to differentiate (`grad`), to
batch (`vmap`), to compile (`jit` → XLA). Because the whole graph is known up front,
the compiler can fuse and schedule it aggressively (the [fusion win](xla-jax.qmd#sec-fusion)).
The cost is the [static-trace constraint](xla-jax.qmd#sec-control-flow): control flow
must go through structured primitives, and shapes drive recompilation. `nitrix` lives
on this side — pure functions, traced and transformed — which is why its differentiability
and its speed come from the same place.

Neither is strictly better; eager flexibility and compiled performance are a genuine
trade. (The frameworks have converged somewhat — PyTorch can compile, JAX is
interactive — but the underlying split is real.)

## Where the paradigm runs out of road {#sec-limits}

A balanced account names the limits, and they motivate the rest of the book.

- **Everything on the gradient path must be (made) differentiable.** The relaxations
  above are approximations, with their own temperatures and biases; some problems
  resist them.
- **Gradients find *local* optima.** Outside the convex case, gradient descent
  reaches a stationary point near where it started, not a global optimum; the result
  depends on initialisation and on the optimiser.
- **Choosing the hypothesis class is the real problem.** Too small a family contains
  no good solution; too large a family fits the noise (overfits) and generalises
  badly. Steering between them — building in the right *inductive bias* — is the
  hardest and most consequential decision, and it is the subject of the
  [final chapter](inductive-bias.qmd).
- **Reverse mode is memory-hungry.** The backward pass needs intermediates from the
  forward pass, so memory grows with depth; gradient checkpointing trades compute to
  bound it.
- **Some outputs are defined by a condition, not a formula.** When a value is the
  solution of an equation, a fixed point, or an optimisation — common in scientific
  computing — differentiating the *solver's* unrolled trace is wasteful and unstable.
  The fix is to differentiate the *condition* instead, via the implicit function
  theorem, which is the [next chapter](implicit-differentiation.qmd).

## What this means for `nitrix`

`nitrix` is, in this language, a library of **hypothesis-class building blocks**: pure,
differentiable primitives from which a scientific computation can be assembled as a
differentiable program and then optimised end-to-end.

- **Composable into a program you can optimise.** Because every primitive is pure and
  traceable, an entire workflow — register, resample, reduce, score — composes into
  one graph that `grad` and `jit` flow through, exactly the loop of @fig-dp-loop.
- **Opaque kernels still participate.** A fused Pallas kernel is not transparent to
  the tracer, so it carries a hand-written `custom_vjp` — its node in the graph
  supplies its own backward rule, keeping the differentiability constraint satisfied.
- **Relaxations are first-class.** The [semiring substrate](three-kernels.qmd#sec-semiring)
  is precisely a knob between a hard combinatorial operation (TROPICAL) and its smooth,
  differentiable relaxation (LOG), and randomness enters through an explicit
  `jax.random` key, the reparameterised form by construction.
- **The two hard limits are the next two chapters.** Solver-defined outputs are
  handled by [implicit differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd); the
  hypothesis-class / inductive-bias problem is the [teaser](inductive-bias.qmd) that
  closes the unit and opens the rest of the book.

The thread is [Thread A](index.qmd#sec-thread-trace): differentiable programming is
what you *do* once you can reinterpret a trace. Compilation made the program fast;
differentiation lets it learn; the paradigm is the discipline of writing programs so
that both are possible.

## Further reading

- Baydin, A. G., Pearlmutter, B. A., Radul, A. A. & Siskind, J. M. (2018).
  "Automatic differentiation in machine learning: a survey." *JMLR* 18. — the
  reference contrast of numerical, symbolic, and automatic differentiation, and the
  computational-graph / Wengert-list view.
- Blondel, M. & Roulet, V. (2024). *The Elements of Differentiable Programming.* — a
  balanced, modern textbook on the paradigm as a whole.
- Kingma, D. P. & Welling, M. (2014). "Auto-encoding variational Bayes." *ICLR.* —
  the reparameterisation trick.
- Bengio, Y., Léonard, N. & Courville, A. (2013). "Estimating or propagating
  gradients through stochastic neurons." — straight-through / surrogate gradients.
- Karpathy, A. (2017). "Software 2.0." — the essay that popularised the
  "specify an objective, learn the program" framing.

## See also

- The trace and its transformations: [JAX and XLA](xla-jax.qmd) (`jit`/`vmap`/`grad`
  as reinterpretations, operator-overloading vs trace-transformation).
- Custom backward rules for opaque kernels: the `custom_vjp`-bearing
  [`_kernels/cuda/`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/tree/main/src/nitrix/_kernels/cuda).
- Relaxations in the substrate: the [semiring product](three-kernels.qmd#sec-semiring).
- Previous chapter: [three kernels](three-kernels.qmd). Next chapters:
  [implicit differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd) (solver-defined outputs)
  and [inductive bias](inductive-bias.qmd) (choosing the hypothesis class).
